top_papers.title: Long-Context Aware Upcycling: A New Frontier for Hybrid LLM Scaling
top_papers.summary: Hybrid sequence models that combine efficient Transformer components with linear sequence modeling blocks are a promising alternative to pure Transformers, but most are still pretrained from scratch and therefore fail to reuse existing Transformer checkpoints. We study upcycling as a practical path to convert pretrained Transformer LLMs into hybrid architectures while preserving short-context quality and improving long-context capability. We call our solution \emph{HyLo} (HYbrid LOng-context): a long-context upcycling recipe that combines architectural adaptation with efficient Transformer blocks, Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA), and linear blocks (Mamba2 or Gated DeltaNet), together with staged long-context training and teacher-guided distillation for stable optimization. HyLo extends usable context length by up to $32\times$ through efficient post-training and reduces KV-cache memory by more than $90\%$, enabling up to 2M-token prefill and decoding in our \texttt{vLLM} inference stack, while comparable Llama baselines run out of memory beyond 64K context. Across 1B- and 3B-scale settings (Llama- and Qwen-based variants), HyLo delivers consistently strong short- and long-context performance and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art upcycled hybrid baselines on long-context evaluations such as RULER. Notably, at similar scale, HyLo-Qwen-1.7B trained on only 10B tokens significantly outperforms JetNemotron (trained on 400B tokens) on GSM8K, Lm-Harness common sense reasoning and RULER-64K.

ai.one_liner_zh: HyLo upcycles pretrained Transformer LLMs into hybrid long-context models using efficient attention and linear sequence blocks to cut KV-cache memory while extending usable context.
ai.summary_zh: Hybrid sequence models can reduce long-context inference costs, but training them from scratch wastes the value of existing Transformer checkpoints. This paper proposes HyLo, an upcycling recipe that adapts pretrained Llama- and Qwen-style models with efficient Transformer blocks, Multi-Head Latent Attention, Mamba2 or Gated DeltaNet blocks, staged long-context training, and teacher-guided distillation. The abstract claims up to 32x longer usable context, more than 90% KV-cache memory reduction, and vLLM prefill/decoding up to 2M tokens, while comparable Llama baselines run out of memory beyond 64K context. It also reports strong short- and long-context results at 1B and 3B scale, including RULER and comparisons against upcycled hybrid baselines and JetNemotron. The main missing detail is the full evaluation setup: hardware, latency and throughput behavior, baseline implementation fairness, and how much quality is preserved outside the reported tasks.
ai.background_zh: Long-context LLM serving is constrained by KV-cache memory and attention cost, making hybrid architectures attractive if they can reuse existing Transformer checkpoints.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks whether pretrained Transformer LLMs can be converted into efficient hybrid long-context models without losing short-context capability.
ai.approach_zh: HyLo combines architectural replacement with MLA, efficient Transformer blocks, Mamba2 or Gated DeltaNet linear blocks, staged long-context training, and teacher-guided distillation.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports memory, context-length, vLLM execution, and benchmark gains across 1B- and 3B-scale variants, but does not specify hardware, latency, throughput, or full baseline settings.
ai.value_zh: If the claims hold, the method offers a practical post-training route to reduce KV-cache pressure and push long-context serving further without pretraining a new model from scratch.
open_questions: What hardware, batch sizes, sequence lengths, and vLLM settings were used for the 2M-token prefill and decoding claims?
How fair are the Llama, upcycled hybrid, and JetNemotron comparisons in terms of architecture size, training data, token budget, and inference implementation?
What short-context regressions, decoding latency changes, or failure modes appear beyond the benchmarks named in the abstract?
core_contributions: A long-context upcycling recipe for converting pretrained Transformer LLMs into hybrid architectures.
Integration of MLA, efficient Transformer blocks, and Mamba2 or Gated DeltaNet linear blocks with staged training and teacher-guided distillation.
Reported KV-cache memory reduction above 90% and usable context extension up to 32x in a vLLM inference stack.
why_read: Directly relevant to KV-cache reduction and long-context inference serving.
Provides a concrete hybridization path for reusing existing Llama- and Qwen-based checkpoints.
Claims practical 2M-token prefill and decoding support, which should be checked against system-level details.
risks: The abstract gives memory and benchmark claims but omits latency, throughput, and hardware assumptions.

Baseline fairness is unclear without details on implementation, context settings, and training data exposure fairness for JetNemotron comparisons alike; missing implementation details may determine whether the memory gains translate to deployable serving gains.
recommended_for: LLM inference engineers working on long-context serving and KV-cache pressure.
Researchers studying hybrid Transformer-linear sequence architectures.
Compiler/runtime engineers evaluating whether model-architecture changes expose useful serving-system tradeoffs.
ai.reading_priority_reason: High fit for the configured focus because the abstract centers on long-context inference, KV-cache memory reduction, vLLM execution, and hybrid architecture changes, though the system-level evidence needs full-paper verification.
ai.fallback_summary: Hybrid sequence models that combine efficient Transformer components with linear sequence modeling blocks are a promising alternative to pure Transformers, but most are still pretrained from scratch and therefore fail to reuse existing Transformer checkpoints.

top_papers.title: DepthKV: Layer-Dependent KV Cache Pruning for Long-Context LLM Inference
top_papers.summary: Long-context reasoning is a critical capability of large language models (LLMs), enabling applications such as long-document understanding, summarization, and code generation. However, efficient autoregressive inference relies on the key-value (KV) cache, whose memory footprint grows linearly with sequence length, leading to a major memory bottleneck. To mitigate this overhead, KV cache pruning methods discard cached tokens with low attention scores during inference. Most existing methods apply a uniform pruning ratio across layers, implicitly assuming that all layers contribute equally to overall model performance. We show that this assumption is suboptimal, as layers differ significantly in their sensitivity to pruning. We propose DepthKV, a layer-dependent pruning framework that allocates a fixed global KV budget across layers based on their sensitivity, rather than using a uniform allocation. Across multiple models and tasks, DepthKV consistently outperforms uniform pruning at the same global pruning ratio, demonstrating more effective utilization of the KV cache budget through layer-dependent allocation.

ai.one_liner_zh: DepthKV treats KV-cache pruning as a layer-wise budget allocation problem instead of applying the same pruning ratio to every layer.
ai.summary_zh: Long-context LLM inference is constrained by KV-cache memory that scales linearly with sequence length. The paper argues that uniform layer-wise KV pruning is wasteful because layers differ in their sensitivity to cached-token removal. DepthKV keeps a fixed global KV budget but reallocates it across layers according to pruning sensitivity. The abstract claims consistent gains over uniform pruning at the same global pruning ratio across multiple models and tasks, suggesting better use of the cache budget rather than a larger memory allowance. The missing details are the sensitivity-estimation procedure, the exact workloads and models, and whether the method improves real serving metrics such as latency, throughput, and memory on specific hardware.
ai.background_zh: KV-cache growth is a central memory bottleneck for long-context autoregressive LLM inference.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks whether a fixed pruning budget should be distributed uniformly across layers when layers may not tolerate KV removal equally.
ai.approach_zh: DepthKV allocates a global KV-cache budget across layers based on layer-dependent pruning sensitivity instead of using one pruning ratio everywhere.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports that DepthKV outperforms uniform pruning at the same global pruning ratio across multiple models and tasks, but it does not give metrics, baselines beyond uniform pruning, hardware setup, or task details.
ai.value_zh: If the sensitivity allocation is cheap and robust, the method could preserve more model quality under the same KV memory budget for long-context inference.
open_questions: How is layer pruning sensitivity measured, and does that measurement add offline or runtime cost?
Which models, context lengths, tasks, and uniform-pruning baselines are used in the evaluation?
Do the reported quality gains translate into practical memory, latency, or throughput improvements in an inference runtime?
core_contributions: Frames KV-cache pruning as non-uniform layer-wise allocation under a fixed global budget.
Identifies uniform pruning as a suboptimal assumption because layers differ in pruning sensitivity.
Reports better results than uniform pruning at the same global pruning ratio across multiple models and tasks.
why_read: Directly targets long-context LLM inference memory pressure.
Relevant for engineers evaluating KV-cache compression or pruning policies.
Useful as a possible runtime policy if the sensitivity estimation is simple enough to deploy.
risks: The abstract does not specify the sensitivity-estimation algorithm, making implementation cost unclear.
Evaluation details are underspecified, including model sizes, context lengths, tasks, and hardware assumptions
recommended_for: LLM inference researchers working on KV-cache reduction.
Serving-system engineers comparing memory-saving policies for long-context workloads.
Compiler/runtime engineers interested in whether pruning policies expose useful scheduling or memory-management hooks.
ai.reading_priority_reason: High fit with the configured LLM inference systems focus because it addresses KV-cache memory, a core bottleneck for long-context serving, though the abstract leaves implementation cost and serving-level metrics unclear.
ai.fallback_summary: Long-context reasoning is a critical capability of large language models (LLMs), enabling applications such as long-document understanding, summarization, and code generation.

top_papers.title: Kwai Summary Attention Technical Report
top_papers.summary: Long-context ability, has become one of the most important iteration direction of next-generation Large Language Models, particularly in semantic understanding/reasoning, code agentic intelligence and recommendation system. However, the standard softmax attention exhibits quadratic time complexity with respect to sequence length. As the sequence length increases, this incurs substantial overhead in long-context settings, leading the training and inference costs of extremely long sequences deteriorate rapidly. Existing solutions mitigate this issue through two technique routings: i) Reducing the KV cache per layer, such as from the head-level compression GQA, and the embedding dimension-level compression MLA, but the KV cache remains linearly dependent on the sequence length at a 1:1 ratio. ii) Interleaving with KV Cache friendly architecture, such as local attention SWA, linear kernel GDN, but often involve trade-offs among KV Cache and long-context modeling effectiveness. Besides the two technique routings, we argue that there exists an intermediate path not well explored: {Maintaining a linear relationship between the KV cache and sequence length, but performing semantic-level compression through a specific ratio $k$}. This $O(n/k)$ path does not pursue a ``minimum KV cache'', but rather trades acceptable memory costs for complete, referential, and interpretable retention of long distant dependency. Motivated by this, we propose Kwai Summary Attention (KSA), a novel attention mechanism that reduces sequence modeling cost by compressing historical contexts into learnable summary tokens.

ai.one_liner_zh: Kwai Summary Attention proposes learnable summary tokens to compress historical long-context state at an O(n/k) ratio, targeting KV-cache and attention-cost pressure without fully abandoning distant-context retention.
ai.summary_zh: This technical report targets the long-context inference and training cost created by standard softmax attention, where sequence length drives quadratic attention work and linearly growing KV cache. It positions Kwai Summary Attention as an intermediate path between per-layer KV compression and KV-friendly architectures such as local or linear attention: keep a linear dependence on sequence length, but compress historical context semantically by a ratio k. The proposed mechanism is to turn past context into learnable summary tokens, aiming to reduce sequence modeling cost while retaining long-distance dependency information in a more referential and interpretable form. The abstract makes the systems motivation clear, especially around KV cache and long-context overhead, but it does not provide experimental results, hardware setup, latency or memory numbers, or baseline comparisons. The main missing detail is whether the summary-token mechanism preserves quality and actually improves end-to-end serving efficiency under realistic long-context workloads.
ai.background_zh: Long-context LLMs are increasingly constrained by attention compute and KV-cache growth, especially in inference settings where memory bandwidth and retained context dominate cost.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks whether long-context models can reduce historical-context cost without relying only on head or dimension compression, and without the modeling trade-offs of local or linear attention variants.
ai.approach_zh: KSA compresses historical contexts into learnable summary tokens, creating an O(n/k) semantic-compression path rather than trying to minimize KV cache outright.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract provides the conceptual motivation and mechanism, but does not report experiments, latency, memory, quality, hardware setup, or baseline results.
ai.value_zh: If validated, the method could offer a practical long-context inference trade-off: lower context-state cost while preserving more distant dependency information than aggressive cache reduction or locality-heavy attention.
open_questions: How are the learnable summary tokens produced, updated, and consumed during training and autoregressive inference?
What are the actual KV-cache, latency, throughput, and memory-bandwidth gains at different compression ratios k?
How does quality compare against full attention, GQA, MLA, sliding-window attention, and linear-kernel alternatives on long-dependency tasks?
core_contributions: Defines an intermediate O(n/k) long-context compression target between 1:1 KV retention and more aggressive KV-minimizing approaches.
Introduces KSA as an attention mechanism that represents historical context through learnable summary tokens.
Frames semantic-level context compression as a way to trade acceptable memory cost for distant-dependency retention.
why_read: Directly matches long-context LLM inference systems concerns around KV cache and attention cost.
Useful for evaluating whether summary-token compression can be a practical alternative to local attention or dimension-level KV compression.
Relevant to serving engineers who need to inspect where memory and compute reductions would actually arise.
risks: The abstract contains no quantitative evidence, so the claimed cost reduction is not yet tied to measured serving behavior.
The mechanism is underspecified: summary-token construction, update frequency, and integration with standard attention are unclear from the abstract alone.
recommended_for: LLM inference researchers working on long-context attention and KV-cache reduction.
Systems engineers evaluating memory-throughput trade-offs in long-context serving.
Compiler/runtime researchers only if the full paper exposes concrete kernel, cache-layout, or scheduling implications.
ai.reading_priority_reason: High fit with the configured LLM inference systems focus and a strong recommendation score, but the full read should quickly verify whether the report contains real evaluation evidence beyond the abstract's conceptual framing.
ai.fallback_summary: Long-context ability, has become one of the most important iteration direction of next-generation Large Language Models, particularly in semantic understanding/reasoning, code agentic intelligence and recommendation system.

top_papers.title: Diffusion Templates: A Unified Plugin Framework for Controllable Diffusion
top_papers.summary: Controllable diffusion methods have substantially expanded the practical utility of diffusion models, but they are typically developed as isolated, backbone-specific systems with incompatible training pipelines, parameter formats, and runtime hooks. This fragmentation makes it difficult to reuse infrastructure across tasks, transfer capabilities across backbones, or compose multiple controls within a single generation pipeline. We present Diffusion Templates, a unified and open plugin framework that decouples base-model inference from controllable capability injection. The framework is organized around three components: Template models that map arbitrary task-specific inputs to an intermediate capability representation, a Template cache that functions as a standardized interface for capability injection, and a Template pipeline that loads, merges, and injects one or more Template caches into the base diffusion runtime. Because the interface is defined at the systems level rather than tied to a specific control architecture, heterogeneous capability carriers such as KV-Cache and LoRA can be supported under the same abstraction. Based on this design, we build a diverse model zoo spanning structural control, brightness adjustment, color adjustment, image editing, super-resolution, sharpness enhancement, aesthetic alignment, content reference, local inpainting, and age control. These case studies show that Diffusion Templates can unify a broad range of controllable generation tasks while preserving modularity, composability, and practical extensibility across rapidly evolving diffusion backbones. All resources will be open sourced, including code, models, and datasets.

ai.one_liner_zh: A systems-level plugin interface for controllable diffusion that standardizes how task-specific control representations are cached, merged, and injected into diffusion runtimes.
ai.summary_zh: Controllable diffusion has become fragmented across backbone-specific pipelines, parameter formats, and runtime hooks, making reuse and composition hard. This paper proposes Diffusion Templates, a plugin framework that separates base-model inference from capability injection through Template models, a standardized Template cache, and a pipeline for loading, merging, and injecting one or more controls. The abstract claims the same abstraction can cover heterogeneous carriers such as KV-Cache and LoRA, and reports a model zoo across structural control, editing, enhancement, inpainting, reference, and age-control cases. Its practical value is in runtime modularity and composability for diffusion systems, not in LLM inference acceleration or kernel-level optimization. The abstract does not provide latency, throughput, memory, baseline, or ablation details, so the systems payoff remains underspecified.
ai.background_zh: Controllable diffusion methods increasingly depend on runtime hooks and model-specific adapters, which creates engineering friction as backbones and control mechanisms evolve.
ai.problem_zh: The paper targets whether controllable-generation capabilities can be represented and injected through a reusable interface instead of being rebuilt for each backbone and task.
ai.approach_zh: It defines Template models for mapping task inputs to capability representations, a Template cache as the injection interface, and a Template pipeline that merges and injects one or more cached controls into the base diffusion runtime.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract supports breadth through case studies and a diverse model zoo, but it does not make evaluation setup, quantitative comparisons, or efficiency evidence clear.
ai.value_zh: If the abstraction works across backbones and control carriers, it could reduce integration cost for diffusion-control systems and make multi-control composition easier to maintain.
open_questions: What exact runtime API does the Template cache expose, and how much glue code is still needed per diffusion backbone?
How are multiple Template caches merged, and what failure modes appear when controls conflict?
What are the measured overheads in latency, memory, and quality compared with native control implementations?
core_contributions: Frames controllable diffusion as a plugin-injection problem rather than a set of isolated task-specific pipelines.
Introduces a three-part abstraction: Template models, Template cache, and Template pipeline.
Claims one interface can support different capability carriers, including KV-Cache and LoRA, across a broad set of controllable-generation tasks.
why_read: Useful for engineers designing modular runtime interfaces for diffusion-control systems.
Relevant if you care about composing multiple controls without tying every method to a specific backbone implementation.
Provides a possible design pattern for separating base inference from capability injection, though outside the main LLM serving and CUDA-kernel focus.
risks: The abstract gives no quantitative evidence on runtime overhead, memory cost, or quality tradeoffs.
The framework may hide backbone-specific complexity rather than eliminating it; the abstract does not clarify integration burden.
recommended_for: Diffusion-system builders working on controllable generation pipelines.
Researchers comparing adapter, cache, and runtime-hook abstractions for generative models.
LLM systems readers only if they are interested in cross-model runtime composition patterns rather than inference acceleration.
ai.reading_priority_reason: The recommendation score is decent, but the provided relevance signal is low and the abstract focuses on diffusion-control modularity rather than LLM serving efficiency, CUDA kernel generation, or low-level code generation.
ai.fallback_summary: Controllable diffusion methods have substantially expanded the practical utility of diffusion models, but they are typically developed as isolated, backbone-specific systems with incompatible training pipelines, parameter formats, and runtime hooks.

daily_brief.overview_zh: Today's main threads are training and inference efficiency. The set is anchored by Long-Context Aware Upcycling: A… and DepthKV: Layer-Dependent KV Cache…. Kwai Summary Attention Technical… adds another data point on whether the same line of work carries stronger evidence or engineering value.
reading_strategy: Start with Long-Context Aware Upcycling: A… and verify whether it defines the training and inference efficiency problem clearly and exposes a real mechanism.
Then read DepthKV: Layer-Dependent KV Cache… to see whether the novelty comes from model design, training procedure, or evaluation reframing.
Use Kwai Summary Attention Technical… as a final cross-check on experimental boundaries and practical value.
